metadata.purpose: Video-based form analysis AI knowledge base

setup.description: Bar placement, stance, and unracking the barbell

checkpoints.high_bar: Bar rests on upper traps, just below C7 vertebra. Elbows tucked under bar to create shelf.
checkpoints.low_bar: Bar rests across rear deltoids, 2-3 inches below high bar position. Creates horizontal lever advantage.

checkpoints.standard: Hip to shoulder-width apart. Toes pointed 15-30 degrees outward. Stance should allow femurs to track over toes throughout descent.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Wider stance reduces ROM and allows greater forward lean — common in equipped and raw powerlifting for max load.

checkpoints.standard: Grip as narrow as shoulder mobility allows. Create full-body tension before unracking. Retract and depress scapulae.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Upper back tightness is critical for bar stability during max effort lifts. Slack in upper back leads to forward collapse.

checkpoints.standard: Minimum steps — typically 2-3. Establish stance before descent. Big breath and brace before initiating.

descent.description: Controlled lowering phase from standing to bottom position

checkpoints.standard: Diaphragmatic breath (360-degree expansion). Valsalva maneuver for max lifts. IAP (intra-abdominal pressure) must be established before descent begins.
Hips break slightly before or simultaneously with knees. Avoid initiating with knees forward — creates excessive anterior shear.
Knees track in line with toes throughout. Neither caving in (valgus) nor collapsing out beyond toes excessively.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Slight intentional knee-out cue ('spread the floor') common in competitive powerlifting to engage glutes and maintain hip external rotation.

checkpoints.standard: High bar: more upright torso. Low bar: greater forward lean is expected and biomechanically appropriate. Spine neutral throughout.
Hip crease at or below top of patella (parallel) for competitive powerlifting standards (IPF/USAPL). Below parallel preferred for full ROM.

bottom_position.description: The transition point — deepest position before ascent

checkpoints.standard: Lumbar spine remains neutral or slightly extended at the bottom. Posterior pelvic tilt (butt wink) indicates mobility limitation or excessive depth beyond mobility.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Mild butt wink tolerated in intermediate lifters, but pronounced tilt under heavy load is an injury risk.

checkpoints.standard: Bar path should remain over midfoot. Any forward drift increases moment arm and compressive forces on knees.

ascent.description: Drive phase from bottom position back to standing

checkpoints.standard: Cue is 'drive the traps into the bar' or 'chest up' to prevent forward collapse. Hips and shoulders should rise at the same rate.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Good morning squat (hips rise faster than shoulders) is a critical fault under max load — shifts load entirely to posterior chain and can cause lumbar injury.

checkpoints.standard: Full hip extension at the top. Glutes squeeze at lockout. No hyperextension of lumbar.
Knees maintain outward tracking. Valgus collapse on ascent is common under fatigue — a fault in competitive context.

common_faults.description: Knees collapse inward during descent or ascent
Hips rise faster than shoulders out of the hole, converting squat into a back-loaded good morning
Posterior pelvic tilt at bottom — lower back rounds under the bar
Hip crease does not reach parallel — results in red light in competition
Excessive forward lean beyond what bar position warrants, usually with upper back rounding

bar_path_analysis.ideal: Vertical bar path over midfoot from side view. Minimal lateral deviation from rear view.

setup.description: Starting position with bar on floor

checkpoints.standard: Bar over midfoot — approximately 1 inch from shins. Not against shins, not far from body.
Hips set at height where back angle is optimal — not a squat, not a stiff-leg. Shins should be near vertical or slightly forward.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Hip height at setup is individual — determined by limb lengths. Taller/longer-femur lifters will have a more horizontal back angle.

checkpoints.standard: Lats pulled down and back before pulling. Cue: 'protect your armpits' or 'bend the bar around your legs'. This prevents bar drift from body.
Double overhand, mixed grip, or hook grip. Hands just outside legs. Bar in fingers, not palms.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Hook grip preferred for competitive lifters for symmetrical pull. Mixed grip acceptable but creates rotational force.

checkpoints.standard: Neutral spine — natural lumbar curve maintained. Not hyper-extended, not rounded. Upper back braced and tight.

pull_initiation.description: Taking slack out and beginning the pull from floor

checkpoints.cue: Taking slack out of the bar
checkpoints.standard: Apply gradual tension to bar before full pull. Bar bends slightly before plates leave floor. Prevents jerking the weight off the floor.
Cue is 'push the floor away' — not 'pull the bar up'. Initiates as a leg press, transitioning to hip hinge as bar passes knees.

ascent.description: Full pull from floor to lockout

checkpoints.standard: Bar travels in a straight vertical line. Bar stays close to body — drags up shins and thighs if necessary.
As bar passes knees, hips drive forward. Transition from leg press to hip extension. Back angle should remain constant until bar passes knees.
Neutral neck — not cranked up or buried in chest. Eyes forward or slightly down. Neutral spine includes cervical spine.

lockout.description: Terminal position at top of lift

checkpoints.standard: Hips fully extended — stand tall. Glutes contracted. No hyperextension of lumbar (leaning back excessively).
checkpoints.competitive_note: Hitching (using thighs to re-shelf bar on ascent) is an IPF/USAPL rules violation. Clean continuous pull required.

checkpoints.standard: Shoulders directly over or slightly behind bar at lockout. Not rolled forward.
Push hips back first as bar descends past hips. Then bend knees as bar passes knees. Reverse of ascent.

common_faults.description: Hips rise faster than the bar — lifter transitions immediately to a stiff-leg position, losing leg drive
Bar swings away from legs during pull, increasing moment arm on lower back dramatically
Lower back rounds during pull — often at initiation or at sticking point
Lifter leans back excessively at top, creating lumbar hyperextension
Bar stalls mid-thigh and lifter re-shelves it on thighs to complete lockout

bar_path_analysis.ideal: Perfectly vertical from side view. Bar stays within 1-2cm of legs throughout pull.

setup.description: Body position, arch, and grip on the bench

checkpoints.standard: Typically 1.5-2x shoulder width. Wrists directly above bar at the bottom. IPF rule: no wider than 81cm ring marks on bar.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Wider grip shortens ROM but increases pec demand and reduces wrist/elbow stress. Individual optimization required.

checkpoints.standard: Natural arch in lower back. Scapulae retracted and depressed — create a tight platform on the bench. This shortens pressing distance and creates stability.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Large arch is legal in most federations and mechanically beneficial. Upper back contact with bench is required in IPF.

checkpoints.standard: Feet flat on floor (preferred for stability and leg drive) or on bench. Leg drive transfers force from floor through body into bar.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Feet flat and actively driving creates legal and effective leg drive. Feet on bench eliminates this advantage.

checkpoints.cue: Bar path over eyes at unrack
checkpoints.standard: Bar should be racked such that at unrack it travels slightly back over the shoulder joint — not forward. Wrist over elbow in starting position.

descent.description: Lowering the bar from arms extended to chest

checkpoints.standard: Elbows at 45-75 degrees from torso — not flared to 90 degrees (shoulder injury risk), not tucked completely (reduces pec involvement).
checkpoints.competitive_note: Optimal angle is individual based on limb length and torso thickness. Generally closer to 45 degrees for bigger arches and wider grips.

checkpoints.standard: Bar touches lower chest — typically nipple line or slightly below depending on arch. Controlled touch, not bouncing.
checkpoints.competitive_note: IPF requires a motionless pause on chest before press command. No bounce.

checkpoints.standard: Wrists neutral — not bent back. Bar in palm with slight wrist extension acceptable if controlled. Excessively bent wrists indicate bar too high in hand.
Bar remains motionless on chest. No sinking, no bouncing, no re-racking. Pause until 'press' command in competition.

press.description: Drive phase from chest back to lockout

checkpoints.standard: Bar travels in a slight arc — back and slightly up toward the face. Not straight up (inefficient) and not forward toward feet.
Drive through legs into the floor as bar is pressed — transfers force through the arch and into the bar. Butt must remain on bench.
checkpoints.competitive_note: Leg drive can add 10-20% to competition bench. Common neglected technique element in intermediate lifters.

checkpoints.standard: Full elbow extension at top. Bar over shoulder joint at lockout. Elbows not hyperextended.

common_faults.description: Elbows flare to 90 degrees from torso — increases anterior shoulder impingement risk
Lifter uses momentum from bounce to complete press — illegal in competition and reduces training stimulus
Hips leave bench during press — a rules violation in all major federations
Bar tilts to one side during press — indicates left/right strength imbalance or inconsistent setup
Elbows not fully locked at completion — red light in competition

bar_path_analysis.ideal: Slight J-curve from side — descent toward lower chest, ascent arcing back toward shoulder joint.

romanian_deadlift.name: Romanian Deadlift (RDL)
romanian_deadlift.context: Accessory lift for posterior chain development. Key hamstring and glute developer. Critical for improving conventional and sumo deadlift strength.

setup.description: Starting from standing with bar in hands

checkpoints.standard: Begin standing with bar at hip crease. Feet hip-width. Slight bend in knees — this knee angle is maintained throughout, not flexed further.
Overhand grip just outside hips. Lats engaged to keep bar close to body throughout. Shoulder blades retracted.

descent.description: Hip hinge lowering bar toward floor

checkpoints.standard: Hips push back, not down. Knee angle stays constant (slight bend only). This is the critical distinction from a deadlift.
Bar stays in contact with or within 1-2cm of legs throughout descent. Drags down thighs toward knees.
Descend until strong hamstring tension is felt. This is the limiting factor — not touching the floor. Depth varies by individual hamstring flexibility.
checkpoints.competitive_note: RDL depth should be limited by hamstring tension, not ego. Forcing past the point of tension leads to lumbar compensation.

checkpoints.standard: Neutral lumbar throughout. Natural lordotic curve maintained. If the back rounds, the descent has gone too far for current flexibility.
Drive hips forward to return to standing. Glutes contract to complete extension. Not a pull with the lower back.
Full hip extension at top. Neutral spine. Glutes contracted. No hyperextension.

common_faults.description: Knees flex significantly during descent, converting hip hinge into a squat pattern
Bar swings away from legs during descent — loads lumbar spinae disproportionately
Lifter continues descent past point of hamstring tension, compensating with lumbar flexion
Lifter leans back beyond neutral at top of movement

bar_path_analysis.ideal: Bar stays within 1-2cm of legs for entire movement. Side view shows straight vertical path as bar descends.

side_view.setup: Camera at hip height, perpendicular to lifter

front_view.setup: Camera at hip height, directly in front of lifter

rear_view.setup: Camera at hip height, directly behind lifter

ipf_squat: Bar loaded with at least 2 collars
Hip crease must descend below top of knee
No stepping forward or backward after unracking — side steps allowed
ipf_deadlift: Bar loaded with at least 2 collars
Lift must be one continuous movement — no hitching
Knees and hips must be in line at lockout
No downward movement of bar before lockout
No bouncing from floor on successive reps in training context
ipf_bench_press: Even extension of both arms to lockout
Butt must remain on bench throughout
Feet must remain in contact with floor or blocks
